intro_band.agent_remit_summary: An airline customer-service system built as three cooperating LLM agents: a triage agent that routes each request by handoff, an FAQ agent that answers airline-policy questions from a curated dataset via faq_lookup_tool, and a seat-booking agent that changes seat assignments via update_seat. Its authorized counterparties are the authenticated customer and the airline's internal reservation and FAQ systems; the only outbound channel is a direct reply in the current session. The remit's defining constraints are hard: identity and confirmation-number ownership MUST be verified against the authoritative reservation record before any seat mutation, authoritative identifiers MUST come from the reservation system rather than being generated at runtime, and every seat change MUST be written to a durable audit log.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python multi-agent example on the OpenAI Agents SDK. examples/customer_service/main.py defines triage_agent, faq_agent, and seat_booking_agent wired with handoffs; tools are faq_lookup_tool (canned keyword lookup) and update_seat. The SDK snapshot under src/agents/ ships a full safety surface — InputGuardrail/OutputGuardrail (guardrail.py), ToolInputGuardrail/ToolOutputGuardrail (tool_guardrails.py), per-tool needs_approval, and handoff input_filter — but the example wires none of them: agents are created with the default empty guardrail lists and needs_approval defaults to False. update_seat writes customer-supplied confirmation number and seat straight into context with no verification, and on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates the flight number with random.randint. Default OpenAI tracing is the only observability, wrapping each turn via trace(group_id=conversation_id).

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is framework-ships-guardrails, example-uses-none: the OpenAI Agents SDK provides input/output guardrails, tool guardrails, and per-tool approval, yet the customer_service example wires zero of them, so every safeguard the remit assumes lives only in prose prompts. The sharpest consequence is update_seat, which mutates reservation state directly from customer-supplied arguments with no identity check, no confirmation-number verification against the authoritative record, and no seat-availability check — any customer can rewrite any reservation by supplying a confirmation number. Compounding it, on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates the authoritative flight number at runtime with random.randint and the mutation depends on that fabricated value. Default tracing supplies baseline observability but no durable, structured audit record of seat changes, and it exports passenger PII to a third-party backend.

rules.rule_text: Requests in this list MUST be declined with a clear explanation and, where appropriate, a pointer to the correct customer-service channel.
Any tool that sends outbound external communication (email, SMS, push, webhook).
Any code-execution, shell, filesystem, or arbitrary-network tool.
Handoff tools only (transfer_to_faq_agent, transfer_to_seat_booking_agent); the triage agent MUST NOT hold any data-access or mutation tool.
Persisting PII, payment data, or reservation details beyond the lifetime of the current session.
Moving reservation or passenger PII to any external channel or service.
Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.
Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human before any reservation is read or modified.
Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.
A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.
A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.
Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — a seat already assigned to another passenger MUST NOT be reassigned.
Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be read or modified.
Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.
Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.
Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.
The FAQ agent MUST answer only from the curated FAQ dataset and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.
Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification; a specialist agent receiving a handoff MUST re-verify identity before any mutation.
The triage agent MUST NOT access or expose customer reservation data directly.
Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system.
Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.
Maximum retries before escalation: a mutation (seat change) that fails verification MUST NOT be retried automatically; escalate instead.

findings.summary: The update_seat tool mutates reservation state directly from customer-supplied arguments with no identity, confirmation-ownership, or seat-availability verification and no approval gate.
findings.description: update_seat writes the customer-typed confirmation number and seat straight into context and returns success, performing none of the remit's required checks — it never confirms the caller's identity, never verifies the confirmation number belongs to the authenticated customer against the authoritative record, and never checks the seat exists or is free. The seat-booking agent also never re-verifies identity after the handoff, and the tool's needs_approval defaults to False, so any customer can rewrite any reservation by supplying a confirmation number. An unscreened customer message (no input guardrail exists, see PRAX-2026-07-17-003) reaches this ungated mutation in one hop.
policy_rule_text: Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human before any reservation is read or modified.
Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.
A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.
A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.
Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — a seat already assigned to another passenger MUST NOT be reassigned.
Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be read or modified.
Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification; a specialist agent receiving a handoff MUST re-verify identity before any mutation.

evidence.snippet: update_seat (lines 67-83) sets context.confirmation_number and context.seat_number from raw args; only assert flight_number is not None — no identity, ownership, or availability check.
FunctionTool.needs_approval defaults to False, so update_seat has no approval interrupt; the SDK's ToolInputGuardrail (tool_guardrails.py) is also unused.

recommended_actions: In update_seat, before mutating context, look up the confirmation number in the authoritative reservation record, confirm it belongs to the authenticated session user, and reject the change if the target seat does not exist or is already assigned.
Gate the tool with a ToolInputGuardrail (or needs_approval) that enforces identity verification on every seat-booking handoff rather than trusting inherited context.

findings.summary: The flight number is fabricated at runtime with random.randint on seat-booking handoff and then treated as an authoritative identifier for the reservation mutation.
findings.description: on_seat_booking_handoff assigns context.flight_number the value f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}" instead of sourcing it from the reservation system, and update_seat's assert flight_number is not None makes that fabricated value a precondition of the seat change. This is exactly the remit's Example Bad Behavior — the system inventing an authoritative identifier and acting on it — so the reservation the mutation appears to operate on is not grounded in any real record.
policy_rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

evidence.snippet: on_seat_booking_handoff (89-91) sets flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}" — a runtime-generated authoritative identifier, not from the reservation system.
update_seat asserts context.context.flight_number is not None, consuming the fabricated flight number as a precondition of the mutation.

recommended_actions: Replace the random flight-number assignment in on_seat_booking_handoff with a lookup that resolves the flight from the authoritative reservation record keyed by the verified confirmation number.
Fail the seat-change routine if the reservation lookup yields no flight, rather than proceeding on a synthesized identifier.

findings.summary: The triage agent runs with no input guardrail, so prompt-injection and off-domain screening exist only in prompt text despite the SDK shipping InputGuardrail.
findings.description: triage_agent is created with the default empty input_guardrails list, and the remit's escalation model explicitly assumes input guardrails on the triage agent to trip on injection or off-domain attempts. The SDK ships InputGuardrail with a tripwire that halts the run, but nothing is wired, so the remit rules that injected instructions in free-text input must not be accepted and that off-scope requests must be declined have no code-level enforcement — only the prompts steer behavior.
policy_rule_text: Requests in this list MUST be declined with a clear explanation and, where appropriate, a pointer to the correct customer-service channel.
Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

evidence.snippet: triage_agent (123-138) is defined with instructions and handoffs only; no input_guardrails argument, so the field defaults to [].
InputGuardrail with tripwire_triggered halts the run, but no InputGuardrail is attached to any agent in the example.

recommended_actions: Attach an InputGuardrail to triage_agent (run_in_parallel=False) that screens each customer message for prompt-injection and off-domain content and trips the tripwire on a hit.
Route tripwire trips and unverifiable-identity cases to the human checkpoint the remit defines rather than continuing the run.

findings.summary: Seat changes are not written to a durable audit log with the remit-required fields; the only record is ephemeral OpenAI trace spans and stdout prints.
findings.description: The remit requires every seat change to be recorded to a durable audit log capturing time, customer identity, confirmation number, old and new seat, and the acting agent. update_seat writes none of this — it returns a string and prints to stdout — and the only structured record is the default OpenAI trace spans, which are third-party, ephemeral, and do not capture the old seat or a verified customer identity. There is no durable, queryable audit trail for the system's highest-risk action.
policy_rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

evidence.snippet: update_seat (67-83) overwrites seat_number with no audit write; no logging call records time, identity, old/new seat, or acting agent.
The only structured record is trace("Customer service", group_id=conversation_id) — OpenAI trace spans, ephemeral and third-party, not an audit log of the required fields.

recommended_actions: Add an explicit audit write in update_seat (or a ToolOutputGuardrail) that records timestamp, verified customer identity, confirmation number, old seat, new seat, and acting agent to a durable, append-only store before returning success.

findings.summary: The FAQ agent's dataset-only grounding is enforced solely by its prompt, with no code gate preventing it from answering from general knowledge.
findings.description: The remit requires the FAQ agent to answer only from the curated dataset and never speculate. faq_lookup_tool is deterministic and returns an explicit "I don't know", but the instruction "Do not rely on your own knowledge" is prompt-only — nothing structurally forces the agent to return the tool output verbatim or to hand back when the tool says it does not know, so the model can still answer from general knowledge. This is a partial control: the deterministic tool exists but its output is not enforced as the sole answer source.
policy_rule_text: The FAQ agent MUST answer only from the curated FAQ dataset and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent instructions say "Use the faq lookup tool ... Do not rely on your own knowledge" — a prompt directive with no code gate on the model's final answer.
faq_lookup_tool returns "I'm sorry, I don't know the answer to that question." but nothing forces the agent to relay that rather than speculate.

recommended_actions: Enforce grounding in code — for example a ToolOutputGuardrail or a wrapper that returns the faq_lookup_tool result directly (or hands back to triage on "I don't know") instead of letting the model compose a free-form answer.

findings.summary: Tool and handoff outputs flow back into agent context unscreened; no tool-output or output guardrail is wired though the SDK ships both.
findings.description: The remit requires that instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — must not be accepted, but every agent is created with an empty output_guardrails list and no ToolOutputGuardrail, so tool results return straight into the model context with no screening. The SDK ships OutputGuardrail and ToolOutputGuardrail (with reject_content / raise_exception behaviors) for exactly this, leaving indirect injection via tool output structurally unmitigated.
policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

evidence.snippet: ToolOutputGuardrail (with reject_content/raise_exception) exists in the SDK but no agent or tool in the example attaches one.
faq_agent and seat_booking_agent are defined with no output_guardrails and no tool_output_guardrails; tool results return into context unscreened.

recommended_actions: Attach a ToolOutputGuardrail to faq_lookup_tool and update_seat that screens returned content for instruction-like text and rejects it (reject_content) before it re-enters the model context.

findings.summary: Default tracing exports passenger PII to the OpenAI tracing backend, a service beyond the remit's inference-only authorization and outliving the session.
findings.description: Tracing is on by default and includes sensitive data by default, so the trace spans the example emits via trace(group_id=conversation_id) carry passenger name, confirmation number, seat, and flight into OpenAI's tracing backend. The remit authorizes the LLM provider for inference only and forbids moving reservation PII to any external service or persisting it beyond the session; the tracing export exceeds both. The same default that earns Monitor credit is a data-movement gap when left unconfigured.
policy_rule_text: Persisting PII, payment data, or reservation details beyond the lifetime of the current session.
Moving reservation or passenger PII to any external channel or service.

evidence.snippet: trace_include_sensitive_data defaults to True (env OPENAI_AGENTS_TRACE_INCLUDE_SENSITIVE_DATA defaults "true"); tracing_disabled defaults False.
with trace("Customer service", group_id=conversation_id) exports turn content — including passenger PII held in context — to the tracing backend.

recommended_actions: For a deployment handling reservation PII, set trace_include_sensitive_data=False (or disable the default trace exporter) so passenger data is not sent to the tracing backend, and route traces to an operator-controlled sink.

findings.summary: The agents specify no model, so the SDK default model is used unpinned, leaving inference behavior undocumented and subject to silent drift.
findings.description: None of triage_agent, faq_agent, or seat_booking_agent set a model, so each resolves to the SDK/runner default at runtime. Dependencies themselves are pinned via a committed uv.lock, but the model — the component whose behavior most directly governs the agents' decisions — has no pin or provenance record in the example, so a default change would silently alter behavior in a customer-facing deployment. This is a supply-chain provenance gap rather than an active exploit.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent, seat_booking_agent, and triage_agent (96-138) are constructed with no model= argument, so the SDK default model is used and is unpinned in the example.

recommended_actions: Pin an explicit model on each Agent (or via a shared RunConfig) for any deployment, and record the model id and provider in an ML-BOM so provenance is auditable.

positives.title: Triage agent holds only handoff tools
positives.description: The triage agent is created with handoff tools only and no data-access or mutation tool, satisfying the remit rule that it must not hold or expose reservation data directly.

positives.title: No forbidden outbound, billing, or code-execution tools present
positives.description: The tool inventory is limited to faq_lookup_tool, update_seat, and handoffs — none of the remit's forbidden external-communication, payment, or shell/exec tools appear in code.

positives.title: Stateless per-session context, no persistence store
positives.description: AirlineAgentContext is an in-memory per-run object with no file or database persistence, matching the remit's prohibition on retaining PII or reservation details beyond the session.

positives.title: Strict JSON-schema validation on tool arguments by default
positives.description: FunctionTool defaults strict_json_schema=True, so tool-call arguments are structurally validated against a strict schema; the example inherits this framework default.

positives.title: Default tracing enabled with per-conversation grouping
positives.description: Tracing is on by default (tracing_disabled=False) and the example wraps each turn in trace(group_id=conversation_id), providing baseline session-grouped observability without extra wiring.

rows.source: agents SDK tracing / Runner via trace() in main.py
rows.content_type: structured trace spans (agent, tool call, handoff), grouped by conversation id
rows.purpose: baseline run observability — records turns, tool calls, and handoffs; captures sensitive data by default

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The system inherits a capable safety framework but activates almost none of it at the example layer, so its behavioral guarantees rest on prompt text rather than code. Its worst exposure is Zero Trust: the one state-mutating tool runs with no identity, ownership, or validity checks and no approval gate. It retains modest credit for a narrow tool inventory, pinned dependencies, and default tracing, but the absence of wired guardrails, a durable audit log, and any adversarial testing keeps every category at or below Partial.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Each agent's prompt scopes it to airline tasks and the tool inventory is narrow and matches the Known Good Baseline, but domain enforcement is prompt-only — the triage agent has no input guardrail (the SDK's InputGuardrail is unused), so off-domain and injection screening has no code gate.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Data sources are controlled and curated (static FAQ dataset, reservation system) with no live-web ingestion, but customer free-text and tool outputs enter agent context unvalidated, FAQ grounding is prompt-only, and default tracing exports passenger PII to a third-party backend.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The single state-mutating tool, update_seat, performs no identity, confirmation-ownership, or seat-availability verification and runs with needs_approval=False; no input, output, or tool guardrails are wired though the SDK ships all of them — only strict JSON-schema arg typing (structural, not authorization) is inherited.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies are pinned and hashed via a committed uv.lock and no credentials sit in the workspace, but the agents specify no model (the SDK default is unpinned in the example) and there is no ML-BOM or model-provenance record.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The SDK repo has an extensive unit-test suite, but there is no evidence of adversarial or injection testing of this customer_service example's security model, and none of the remit's identity/injection risk paths are exercised by a test that drove a design change.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Default OpenAI tracing wraps each turn and groups spans by conversation id, giving real baseline observability, but there is no durable, structured audit log of seat mutations capturing the remit-required fields — the trace backend is ephemeral, third-party, and not an audit record.
